primary_diagnosis.name: Exercise-associated muscle cramp of the right medial gastrocnemius (benign exertional skeletal muscle cramp)

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden painful involuntary contraction of a single muscle immediately after prolonged exertion with heavy sweating — the classic mechanism.

differential.diagnosis: deep vein thrombosis of the calf
differential.evidence_basis: Unilateral calf pain always raises DVT; must be actively risk-stratified and excluded on history because it is limb- and life-threatening (PE).
Severe post-exertional calf pain with a tense, hard muscle can signal compartment syndrome — a surgical emergency; screen for pain out of proportion, paraesthesia, and pulselessness.
A cold, pale, pulseless, numb limb would indicate arterial occlusion; must be screened even though unlikely in a fit runner.
A sudden pop with focal calf pain post-exertion can be a medial gastrocnemius tear; distinguished by a palpable defect/gap and pain that does not fluctuate with an active spasm.

Instantaneous onset at peak exertion, brief relief on passive stretch with recurrence at rest, a discrete palpable muscle knot, heavy sweating with plain-water rehydration, and intact sensation/movement — a textbook benign cramp.

differential.diagnosis: deep vein thrombosis of the calf
differential.evidence_basis: Low pre-test probability (low Wells): instantaneous exertional onset rather than gradual, a focal knot rather than diffuse swelling/erythema/warmth, no VTE risk factors and an active runner. Cannot be confirmed excluded by imaging in telehealth — retain as a safety-net trigger.

Reassured by intact sensation/movement and relief on stretch, but the tense woody compartment, pulses, and true pain-out-of-proportion cannot be assessed remotely — kept as an active safety-net trigger rather than fully excluded.
Micro-tearing may coexist as a sequela of the violent contraction; the fluctuating spasm pattern and absence of a reported palpable gap make a major tear less likely, but delayed soreness overlaps.

Benign self-limiting exertional cramp; management is stretch, rehydration with electrolytes, simple analgesia and safety-netting. Serious vascular/compartment mimics are clinically unlikely but cannot be fully excluded remotely, so conservative safety-netting is mandatory.

red_flags.description: Whole-leg uniform swelling, redness, warmth, or a persistent constant ache replacing the cramp — features of DVT (or cellulitis).
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise DVT pre-test probability substantially; DVT can propagate and embolise to the lungs.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed DVT progressing to pulmonary embolism — potentially fatal.

red_flags.description: Escalating pain out of proportion, a tense woody swollen compartment, numbness/pins-and-needles, or inability to move the toes — features of compartment syndrome or arterial ischaemia.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Compartment syndrome and acute limb ischaemia are limb-threatening surgical emergencies with a narrow time window.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Irreversible muscle/nerve necrosis and possible limb loss.

red_flags.description: Dark tea- or cola-coloured urine, markedly reduced urine output, or systemic unwellness — features of rhabdomyolysis after severe exertional muscle injury.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe or prolonged muscle contraction can release myoglobin, causing acute kidney injury.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed rhabdomyolysis leading to acute renal failure.

red_flags.description: New chest pain, breathlessness, or coughing up blood — features of pulmonary embolism.
red_flags.clinical_significance: PE is the lethal complication of an unrecognised DVT.

pathophysiology_summary: Exercise-associated muscle cramps are sudden, involuntary, painful contractions of a skeletal muscle (here the medial head of the gastrocnemius) occurring during or immediately after strenuous exertion. The leading mechanism is altered neuromuscular control from muscle fatigue — reduced inhibitory (Golgi tendon organ) and increased excitatory (muscle spindle) afferent activity producing sustained alpha-motor-neuron firing — compounded in this case by dehydration and electrolyte shift from heavy sweating with plain-water replacement. Passive stretch relieves the cramp by increasing Golgi tendon organ inhibitory feedback, breaking the reflex loop; the contraction recurs on rest while the muscle remains fatigued and under-hydrated. The condition is benign and self-limiting; the clinical task is to distinguish it from serious mimics of acute unilateral calf pain (DVT, compartment syndrome, arterial ischaemia, significant muscle tear) and to prevent recurrence and the rare complication of rhabdomyolysis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on severe unilateral calf pain equals DVT/compartment syndrome and reflexively escalating a textbook exertional cramp to an urgent in-person or ED assessment. The source note itself did this; the discriminating history resolves to a benign cramp that is telehealth-manageable with self-care and safety-netting.

The opposite error: correctly identifying a cramp but discharging with pure reassurance and no safety-net, so an evolving DVT, compartment syndrome, or rhabdomyolysis is missed. Because pulses, the compartment, and imaging cannot be assessed remotely, robust conditional safety-netting is mandatory.

Recommending quinine for cramp. Quinine is not indicated for exercise-associated cramps and carries a serious risk of thrombocytopenia and cardiac arrhythmia (TGA/FDA warnings); it is an error of commission.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Ongoing NSAID in a dehydrated post-exertional patient
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Continuing ibuprofen/NSAIDs in a dehydrated runner with possible exertional muscle breakdown adds renal risk (NSAID plus dehydration plus possible rhabdomyolysis); paracetamol is the safer analgesic here.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Distal pulses (dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial) — bounding and equal on the in-person exam
Capillary refill time (<2 seconds on exam)
Whether the compartment is tense/woody by palpation and the true degree of pain on passive stretch
Direct palpation of the focal rock-hard spasm and confirmation of no palpable muscle-belly defect
Formal neurological examination (sensation, reflexes) of the lower limb
Serum electrolytes and creatine kinase (only useful in recurrent/severe cases; not acutely indicated)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the classic exercise-associated cramp, give self-care (assisted stretching, electrolyte rehydration, simple analgesia, heat, relative rest) and STRONG conditional safety-netting for DVT / compartment syndrome / rhabdomyolysis / PE; arrange GP or physiotherapy review in 3-5 days if pain persists. Do NOT reflexively refer a textbook cramp to ED, and do NOT dismiss without safety-netting.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis itself (the constellation is classic). The real risks are OVER-action (unnecessary urgent in-person/ED referral) and, less commonly, UNDER-action (reassurance without safety-netting for the serious mimics that cannot be excluded remotely).
